stage.name: Ordering and fences: memory-path checkpoint
stage.description: A choice-only checkpoint that ties together store commit, load alias checks, translation caches, SFENCE.VMA, PMA, cache boundaries, and source-scoped fence behavior.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

scenes.title: Memory ordering is where shortcuts break

content.title: Ordering and fences: memory-path checkpoint
content.subtitle: This checkpoint ties together store commit, load aliasing, PMA, translation caches, and fence-related source evidence.
content.thesis: The safest memory explanation names which ordering problem is being solved and which source supports the claim.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 deprecated design docs: store buffer
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The store buffer tracks speculative and committed store queues; speculative entries are flushed while committed entries persist.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A6 CSRs: satp and SFENCE.VMA note
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Writing satp does not by itself order page-table updates or invalidate translation caches; SFENCE.VMA may be required.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: PMA
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 PMA regions include non-idempotent, executable, and cacheable regions; standalone CVA6 does not define a coherence protocol.

content.speakerNotes: This lesson closes Unit 07 with ordering and fences. Memory ordering is where shallow explanations become dangerous. A store buffer solves one kind of problem. SFENCE.VMA solves a translation-cache ordering problem. PMA solves a region-semantics problem. Cache flush signals solve configuration-scoped control problems. These are related, but they are not one magic word called memory.

actions.text: This lesson closes Unit 07 with ordering and fences. Memory ordering is where shallow explanations become dangerous. A store buffer solves one kind of problem. SFENCE.VMA solves a translation-cache ordering problem. PMA solves a region-semantics problem. Cache flush signals solve configuration-scoped control problems. These are related, but they are not one magic word called memory.

scenes.title: Do not collapse every ordering problem into one bucket

content.title: Four memory-ordering questions to classify
content.subtitle: A useful checkpoint starts by naming the exact problem.

cards.title: Store visibility
cards.body: Has the speculative store become a committed store, and has the oldest committed store reached memory?

cards.title: Load freshness
cards.body: Could a younger load read stale data because an older store with matching page offset is unresolved?

cards.title: Translation freshness
cards.body: Have page-table changes and translation-cache invalidation been ordered with SFENCE.VMA when needed?

cards.title: Region semantics
cards.body: Is the target region non-idempotent, executable, cacheable, coherent, or delegated to the system?

content.takeaway: Different memory hazards need different explanations.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 deprecated design docs: store buffer
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The store buffer tracks speculative and committed store queues; speculative entries are flushed while committed entries persist.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A6 CSRs: satp and SFENCE.VMA note
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Writing satp does not by itself order page-table updates or invalidate translation caches; SFENCE.VMA may be required.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: PMA
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 PMA regions include non-idempotent, executable, and cacheable regions; standalone CVA6 does not define a coherence protocol.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A65X design: controller fence and cache flush signals
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The controller exposes cache flush and fence-related wiring; several fence and TLB-flush signals are tied off in this configuration.

content.speakerNotes: The first checkpoint skill is classification. Store visibility asks about commit and the store buffer. Load freshness asks about alias checks. Translation freshness asks about satp, page tables, TLBs, and SFENCE.VMA. Region semantics asks about PMA and system boundaries. If a learner can classify the problem, they can usually find the right source.

actions.text: The first checkpoint skill is classification. Store visibility asks about commit and the store buffer. Load freshness asks about alias checks. Translation freshness asks about satp, page tables, TLBs, and SFENCE.VMA. Region semantics asks about PMA and system boundaries. If a learner can classify the problem, they can usually find the right source.

content.title: Do not teach SFENCE.VMA as a generic data-cache broom
content.subtitle: The satp note is specifically about page-table updates and address-translation caches.

columns.title: What it addresses
columns.body: Software may need SFENCE.VMA after page-table updates or ASID reuse so later translations observe the intended mapping.

columns.title: What not to claim
columns.body: Do not describe it as a universal data-cache flush or as proof of every fence behavior in every CVA6 configuration.

content.takeaway: A fence has a target; name that target.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A6 CSRs: satp and SFENCE.VMA note
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Writing satp does not by itself order page-table updates or invalidate translation caches; SFENCE.VMA may be required.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 Programmer View: RISC-V virtual memory
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 virtual memory depends on MMUEn and XLEN; CV32A6 supports Sv32, CV64A6 supports Sv39, and Bare mode is the default.

content.speakerNotes: SFENCE.VMA deserves a precise explanation. The CSR note says writing satp alone does not imply ordering constraints between page-table updates and subsequent translations, nor does it imply invalidation of address-translation caches. That is very specific. It is about translation freshness. It is not a hand-wave for every possible memory effect.

actions.text: SFENCE.VMA deserves a precise explanation. The CSR note says writing satp alone does not imply ordering constraints between page-table updates and subsequent translations, nor does it imply invalidation of address-translation caches. That is very specific. It is about translation freshness. It is not a hand-wave for every possible memory effect.

scenes.title: Checkpoint map: from load/store to translation to system

content.title: Follow the memory claim outward
content.body: Start inside the LSU, move through load/store ordering and translation, then cross the cache/PMA boundary into system-level behavior only when the source supports it.
content.caption: Use the scope diagram as the final check before making system-level claims.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A65X design: load_unit
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The load unit checks the store buffer before issuing a load and stalls load-after-store cases when the older store has not reached the cache.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: PMA
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 PMA regions include non-idempotent, executable, and cacheable regions; standalone CVA6 does not define a coherence protocol.

content.speakerNotes: This is the map I want learners to keep. Start with the instruction and the LSU. Then ask whether the problem is load/store ordering, address translation, cache interaction, or system memory attributes. Only after that should we make a claim about system-level behavior. This avoids both under-teaching and over-promising.

actions.text: This is the map I want learners to keep. Start with the instruction and the LSU. Then ask whether the problem is load/store ordering, address translation, cache interaction, or system memory attributes. Only after that should we make a claim about system-level behavior. This avoids both under-teaching and over-promising.

questions.question: A store has been issued but has not reached commit. What is the safest description?

options.label: It has definitely changed architectural memory
It is tracked as speculative store intent and must wait for commit discipline
It is a frontend prediction event
It cannot be flushed under any condition

questions.analysis: The store buffer separates speculative outstanding stores from committed stores.

questions.question: A younger load has a matching page offset with an older unresolved store. What does the documented no-forwarding path imply?

options.label: The load may stall to avoid stale data
The load always receives forwarded data
The branch predictor chooses the data

questions.analysis: Without store-to-load forwarding, the load waits when the older store may be the needed value.

questions.question: Which facts belong to the address-translation freshness problem?
questions.analysis: Multiplication latency is an execute-unit timing topic, not a translation-cache freshness issue.

questions.question: Which PMA region type is especially risky for speculative reads?
questions.analysis: Non-idempotent regions can have reads that destroy or change state, so speculation must be controlled.

questions.question: How should tied-off fence or TLB-flush signals in a configuration be interpreted?

options.label: As configuration-scoped source evidence, not universal behavior
As a license to invent behavior
As proof the LSU never handles memory
As a replacement for all PMA discussion

questions.analysis: Signal interpretation must stay scoped to the documented configuration.

questions.question: Which boundaries should a professional CVA6 memory lesson keep separate?
questions.analysis: The first three are technical boundaries; the fourth is not part of memory correctness.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 deprecated design docs: store buffer
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The store buffer tracks speculative and committed store queues; speculative entries are flushed while committed entries persist.

sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Writing satp does not by itself order page-table updates or invalidate translation caches; SFENCE.VMA may be required.

content.speakerNotes: This checkpoint is deliberately classification-heavy. The goal is not trivia. The goal is to decide whether a case is about store commit, load aliasing, translation freshness, PMA region semantics, or configuration-scoped fence behavior. That is the kind of memory literacy that survives contact with real RTL.

actions.text: This checkpoint is deliberately classification-heavy. The goal is not trivia. The goal is to decide whether a case is about store commit, load aliasing, translation freshness, PMA region semantics, or configuration-scoped fence behavior. That is the kind of memory literacy that survives contact with real RTL.

content.title: You can now read the CVA6 memory path as layered contracts
takeaways: LSU explains load/store execution, exceptions, and cache-facing requests.
Store buffer and alias checks explain why some memory operations wait.
MMU, TLB, PTW, SFENCE.VMA, and PMA explain address and region correctness.
content.nextStep: Next unit: interfaces and integration, where AXI, CV-X-IF, interrupts, debug, and SoC responsibilities become the focus.
content.closing: Memory is not one mechanism. It is a stack of contracts.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A65X design: load_store_unit
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The LSU interfaces with the data cache, handles load/store operations, reports load and store results or exceptions, and connects to commit and PMP configuration.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: PMA
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 PMA regions include non-idempotent, executable, and cacheable regions; standalone CVA6 does not define a coherence protocol.

content.speakerNotes: Unit 07 is complete. You can now read CVA6 memory as layered contracts. The LSU handles load and store execution. The store buffer and alias checks preserve correctness. The MMU and PTW translate addresses or raise faults. SFENCE.VMA protects translation freshness. PMA labels region behavior. Next we move outward into interfaces and integration.

actions.text: Unit 07 is complete. You can now read CVA6 memory as layered contracts. The LSU handles load and store execution. The store buffer and alias checks preserve correctness. The MMU and PTW translate addresses or raise faults. SFENCE.VMA protects translation freshness. PMA labels region behavior. Next we move outward into interfaces and integration.
